channel.title: NYT > Books > Book Review
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Book Review: ‘This Vast Enterprise,’ by Craig Fehrman.
item.description: In “This Vast Enterprise,” Craig Fehrman refreshes a familiar story with a rich chorus of voices.
item.dc:creator: Andrea Wulf
item.category: This Vast Enterprise: A New History of Lewis & Clark (Book)

item.title: Book Review: ‘When We See You Again,’ by Rachel Goldberg-Polin
item.description: Rachel Goldberg-Polin’s precise and devastating memoir chronicles the 328 days her son was held hostage in Gaza, and what came after.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Jacobs
item.media:description: Rachel Goldberg-Polin during her remarks at the 2024 Democratic National Convention, where she called for help in getting her son released after 320 days.

item.title: Book Review: ‘How to Be a Dissident,’ by Gal Beckerman
item.description: In “How to Be a Dissident,” Gal Beckerman offers an inspiring tour of famous renegades with lessons for the rabble-rousers of today.
item.dc:creator: Astra Taylor
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations

item.title: Book Review: ‘On the Calculation of Volume IV,’ by Solvej Balle
item.description: Solvej Balle’s cult hit series about a woman trapped in a time loop continues with a fourth volume.
item.dc:creator: A.O. Scott

item.title: The 10 Best Books of 2025
item.description: The staff of The New York Times Book Review choose the year’s top fiction and nonfiction.
item.category: The Loneliness of Sonia and Sunny (Book)
There Is No Place for Us: Working and Homeless in America (Book)
Mother Emanuel: Two Centuries of Race, Resistance, and Forgiveness in One Charleston Church (Book)
A Marriage at Sea: A True Story of Love, Obsession, and Shipwreck (Book)
Wild Thing: A Life of Paul Gauguin (Book)

item.title: Gwendoline Riley Would Prefer You Resist Assuming Her Life Is Like Her Books
item.description: The British author Gwendoline Riley may be as emotionally guarded as the women in her novels, which have caught on in America.
item.dc:creator: Thessaly La Force
item.media:credit: Billy Barraclough for The New York Times
item.media:description: The writer Gwendoline Riley in London ahead of the publication of her new novel, “The Palm House.”

item.title: Great Books to Bring Young Readers Into the Wilderness
item.description: The author of “A Wolf Called Wander” recommends titles old and new, fantastical and true, that celebrate the natural world.
item.dc:creator: Rosanne Parry
item.category: The Forest of a Thousand Eyes (Book)
The Unlikely Hero: The Story of Wolf 8 (Book)

item.title: Lena Dunham Takes to Her Bed to Promote Her Memoir, “Famesick.”
item.description: Forget demure conversations in spindly chairs. To promote “Famesick,” a new memoir, she’s taken to her bed and invited friends to jump in. Onstage.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Egan
item.media:credit: Nina Westervelt for The New York Times
item.media:description: Lena Dunham and Andrew Rannells during the “Famesick” book tour stop at the Brooklyn Academy of Music.

item.title: Enchanting New Fantasy Books
item.description: Our columnist reviews this season’s new books.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Harlan

item.title: Books Our Editors Loved This Week
item.description: Reading recommendations from critics and editors at The New York Times.
item.category: Korean Messiah: Kim Il Sung and the Christian Roots of North Korea's Personality Cult (Book)
Rasputin: The Downfall of the Romanovs (Book)
The Future Is Peace: A Shared Journey Across the Holy Land (Book)

item.title: Book Review: ‘EXTRA SAUCE’ by Zahra Tangorra, ‘ON EATING' by Alicia Kennedy
item.description: Both authors share uncanny similarities of upbringing. But their culinary paths diverged sharply.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Reese
item.category: Extra Sauce: The Good, the Bad, and the Onions (Book)
On Eating: The Making and Unmaking of My Appetites (Book)
item.media:credit: Ye Fan for The New York Times
item.media:description: For both authors, food was formative. But it shaped their lives in different ways.

item.title: Book Review: ‘Dear Monica Lewinsky,’ by Julia Langbein
item.description: Julia Langbein’s novel considers the legions of women whose lives have been forever marred by compromising early relationships.
item.dc:creator: Zoe Dubno

item.title: Interview: Arthur Sze on Translating Poetry and His Favorite Books
item.description: The U.S. poet laureate’s new book, “Transient Worlds,” collects 23 poems in 13 languages to show the many ways a work can be translated.

item.title: Book Review: ‘The Violence,’ by Adriana E. Ramírez
item.description: Through accounts of relatives and direct witnesses, Adriana E. Ramírez examines a pivotal, and brutal, period of history.
item.dc:creator: Annie Correal
item.category: The Violence: My Family's Colombian War (Book)

item.title: Barbara Gordon, 90, Dies; Wrote a Best Seller About Her Pill Addiction
item.description: Her 1979 memoir, “I’m Dancing as Fast as I Can,” which also became a movie, detailed years of prescription drug abuse and offered an indictment of American psychiatry.
item.dc:creator: Trip Gabriel
item.category: Im Dancing as Fast as I Can (Book)

item.title: ‘Giant’ Revisits Roald Dahl’s Antisemitic Comments: What to Know
item.description: Mark Rosenblatt’s Broadway play, starring John Lithgow as the British children’s book author, draws from Dahl’s comments over the years.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr
item.media:credit: Ronald Dumont/Daily Express, via Hulton Archive, via Getty Images
item.media:description: The British author Roald Dahl in 1971.

item.title: Book Review: ‘Muskism,’ by Quinn Slobodian and Ben Tarnoff
item.description: In a new book, Quinn Slobodian and Ben Tarnoff argue that Elon Musk’s disruptive approach to business is transforming both politics and the economy.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Szalai
item.category: Muskism: A Guide for the Perplexed (Book)
item.media:description: Elon Musk visits a Tesla plant in Germany in 2024. Muskism, like Fordism, is not an individual but a system.

item.title: Book Review: ‘Rasputin’ by Antony Beevor
item.description: In “Rasputin,” the biographer Antony Beevor delves into the mysterious life of the last czarina’s mystic adviser.
item.dc:creator: Sophie Pinkham
item.category: Rasputin: The Downfall of the Romanovs (Book)
item.media:description: Despite — or because of — the fact that he left behind almost no writings, Grigori Rasputin remains one of the most enigmatic figures of modern history.

item.title: Book Review: ‘Into the Wood Chipper,’ by Nicholas Enrich
item.description: Nicholas Enrich’s tell-all memoir, “Into the Wood Chipper,” has advice for others caught between their conscience and their government.
item.dc:creator: Janice P. Nimura
item.category: Into the Wood Chipper: A Whistleblower's Account of How the Trump Administration Shredded USAID (Book)
United States Agency for International Development
item.media:description: Packages containing H.I.V. drugs in a medical stockroom in Kenya, part of the last donation from the U.S. Agency for International Development before it was shut down last year.

item.title: Obsessed With the Titanic? These Historical Fictional Books Will Transport You.
item.description: This gripping historical fiction will transport you to the doomed ship and back to land.
item.dc:creator: Donna Jones Alward

item.title: In ‘Famesick,’ Lena Dunham Diagnoses Celebrity, Illness and Herself
item.description: This unusually unfiltered memoir takes us to the hospital, to therapy and to the sometimes hostile set of “Girls.”
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Jacobs
item.media:credit: OK McCausland for The New York Times
item.media:description: In “Famesick,” Lena Dunham describes how she and her often-fragile body have been harshly scrutinized.

item.title: Book Review: ‘RFK Jr.,’ by Isabel Vincent
item.description: In “RFK Jr.: The Fall and Rise,” a New York Post reporter paints an intimate portrait of the Kennedy scion and cabinet member.
item.dc:creator: Nicolas Niarchos
item.category: RFK Jr: The Fall and Rise (Book)
item.media:credit: George De Sota/Newsmakers, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Robert F. Kennedy Jr. attends a benefit for the environmental group Riverkeeper in New York City in 2001.

item.title: Book Review: ‘Korean Messiah,’ by Jonathan Cheng
item.description: A new history by Jonathan Cheng argues that an influx of missionaries in the late 19th century profoundly shaped the ruling Kim family dynasty.
item.dc:creator: Bruce Cumings
item.category: Korean Messiah: Kim Il Sung and the Christian Roots of North Korea's Personality Cult (Book)
item.media:description: The American evangelist Billy Graham presents his book “Peace With God” to the North Korean leader Kim Il-sung in April 1992 in Pyongyang.

item.title: Book Review: ‘Where the Music Had to Go,’ by Jim Windolf
item.description: Jim Windolf’s new book, “Where the Music Had to Go,” traces the influence of Dylan on the Beatles and the Beatles on Dylan.
item.dc:creator: Francine Prose
item.category: Where the Music Had to Go: How Bob Dylan and the Beatles Changed Each Other -- and the World (Book)
item.media:credit: Clay Hickson; Photographs by Jan Persson/Redfern and Bob Whitaker, via Getty Images

item.title: Tucker Carlson Is Starting a Publishing Imprint
item.description: Tucker Carlson Books, a joint venture between Carlson’s media company and Skyhorse Publishing, will put out books by Russell Brand, Milo Yiannopoulos and more.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Alter
item.media:description: Tucker Carlson’s new publishing imprint will further expand the influence of its parent company, Skyhorse Publishing, in the conservative publishing space

item.title: Book Review: ‘See You on the Other Side,’ by Jay McInerney
item.description: Jay McInerney has written about the literary party boy Russell Calloway once a decade since the 1990s. He returns in the Covid novel “See You on the Other Side.”
item.dc:creator: Dwight Garner
item.category: See You on the Other Side (Book)
item.media:credit: Rebecca Smeyne for The New York Times

item.title: Book Review: ‘Go Gentle,’ by Maria Semple
item.description: Maria Semple is back with another frenetic human comedy.
item.dc:creator: Jeff Giles

item.title: Book Review: ‘The Future Is Peace,’ by Aziz Abu Sarah and Maoz Inon
item.description: Aziz Abu Sarah and Maoz Inon both lost loved ones to the conflict in the Middle East. In “The Future Is Peace,” they look for hope and understanding.
item.dc:creator: Fintan O’Toole
item.category: The Future Is Peace: A Shared Journey Across the Holy Land (Book)
item.media:description: Aziz Abu Sarah, center, and Maoz Inon, right, meet with Pope Leo XIV at the Vatican in 2025.
